title: Max Avramis
text:
javramis@ucsd.edu

LinkedIn · GitHub

Education
UC San Diego, GPS

Bachelor of Fine Arts · 2020

Skills

RPythonQGISGoogle Earth EngineSQLGitHubQuartoggplot2caretmetafortidyverseXGBoostRandom ForestExcelSharePoint

Languages

English (native)Spanish (intermediate)Greek (beginner)





I came to policy school from a career as a professional actor, which left me with a particular sensitivity to audience — to whether an idea is actually being communicated, not just being said.
What really drew me into GIS and data science was realizing these tools could do something no other analytical framework could: make a rigorous, multi-variable argument legible to essentially anyone in any room. I had spent a year building a foundation in quantitative policy analysis — cost-effectiveness, benefit-cost analysis, and my personal favorite, multi-criteria decision analysis. GIS gave me the communication layer. Combining them felt like gaining a universal language.
That instinct runs through everything I build: the question is never just “is this accurate?” but “will this land for the person making the decision?”




My work sits at the intersection of spatial analysis, machine learning, and policy research — with a particular interest in:

Climate and disaster risk mapping (flood hazards, wildfire vulnerability)
Global health infrastructure and pandemic preparedness
Education technology and learning inequality
Labor markets and AI regulation





Health Forecasting Alliance (VitalWave) · Strategy & Research Consultant · Jan 2026–Present

title: Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events
text:
← Back to Research



Course: Big Data: Policy, Governing & Management Instructor: Prof. Rod Albuyeh Term: Fall 2025 Tools: R · caret · XGBoost · Random Forest · pROC
:::

title: Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events
text:
The Question
Can we predict whether a U.S. labor action event will be classified as a strike versus a protest — and which economic and geographic conditions are most predictive?

title: Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events
text:
Data
Labor Action Tracker (LAT) from Cornell’s School of Industrial Labor Relations, covering 2021–2025. Merged at ZIP/ZCTA level with:

DCI — Distressed Communities Index (2015–2023)
SDI — Social Deprivation Index (2015–2019)
IRS tax statistics — 2022 income aggregates by ZIP

Final modeling dataset: thousands of events with complete predictor coverage after geographic join validation (>98% ZCTA coverage).

title: Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events
text:
Methodology
Data pipeline highlights: - Temporal train/test split (train: pre-Dec 2023; test: Dec 2023–Jan 2025) — never let future data touch training - Saved factor levels exclusively from training data and applied consistently to test set - Only filtered on missing outcome variable — never on predictors - Built 20+ binary industry indicators from semicolon-delimited tags - Log-transformed participant counts with median imputation on training data only
Models compared:



Model
Approach




Logistic Regression
Baseline interpretable model


Random Forest
Ensemble, handles nonlinearity


XGBoost
Gradient boosting, best AUC



Evaluation: AUC/ROC curves, confusion matrices, variable importance plots.

title: Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events
text:
Key Outputs

[ROC curves comparing three models — add your image here] ::: {.visual-caption} ROC curves for logistic regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost on held-out test set.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
← Back to Research



Course: GIS & Spatial Data Analysis Instructor: Prof. Gordon McCord Term: Fall 2025 Tools: QGIS · R · MCDA

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
The Question
Which municipalities in the Attica region of Greece face the greatest combined wildfire vulnerability — and where should infrastructure investment be prioritized under Greece’s €2.1 billion AEGIS civil protection program?

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Why It Matters
On July 23, 2018, the coastal village of Mati became the site of Europe’s deadliest wildfire since 1900. Within hours, 104 people were dead — many found meters from the sea, having fled but unable to escape in time. The post-incident investigation revealed not just extreme weather conditions, but fundamental failures in emergency response coordination: fire service officials were later convicted of criminal negligence for failing to recommend evacuations or warn the public.
Mati exposed a critical spatial disconnect — between where fires occur and where emergency response capacity exists. In 2023 alone, wildfires caused approximately €1.8 billion in damages across Greece, nearly half of Europe’s total wildfire losses that year. As climate projections indicate southern and eastern Greece will experience up to 40 additional days of high fire danger annually relative to the late 20th century, the case for evidence-based infrastructure planning is urgent.
Greece’s response — the €2.1 billion AEGIS program, announced in 2024 — represents the country’s largest-ever civil protection procurement, acquiring over 1,000 firefighting vehicles, amphibious aircraft, drones, and fire control centers. But the spatial question remains: where should this infrastructure go?

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Data Sources







Dataset
Source




Municipality boundaries + population density
Eurostat GISCO 2024 LAU dataset


Wildfire incidents 2000–2024
DataForGreece + Hellenic Fire Service


Fire station locations
Geofabrik OpenStreetMap POI shapefile


Regional boundary
GADM 4.1 (gadm41_GRC_2 “Attica”)

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Methodology

1. Fire Exposure by Municipality
Individual fire incident points were aggregated to municipality boundaries using QGIS “Join attributes by location (summary)” — effectively a zonal statistics operation. Three metrics were computed per municipality: total fire incidents, cumulative burned area (hectares), and mean fire severity per incident. These were combined into a composite fire exposure score and classified into five graduated categories.


2. Emergency Service Access
Municipality centroids were computed and distances to nearest fire station calculated using QGIS “Distance to nearest hub (points).” A binary AccessClass variable was created via field calculator: municipalities more than 10km from the nearest station were classified as “Under-served.” The 10km threshold reflects wildfire suppression research establishing that the first 10–30 minutes of initial attack are the most critical — at typical emergency response speeds, 10km represents the difference between arriving when fires are still containable versus arriving when they’ve already grown beyond initial attack capability.


3. Multi-Criteria Priority Classification
The final MCDA step integrated all three dimensions using a CASE expression in the field calculator. Municipalities were flagged as priority if they simultaneously met: cumulative burned area in the upper quartile of the regional distribution, AccessClass = “Under-served (>10km),” and population density above the regional median. This ensures prioritization reflects fire risk, infrastructure gaps, and the number of residents affected.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Results


Composite fire exposure classification across Attica municipalities (2000–2024). Dark red = Very High exposure. Fire incident points overlaid. Green diamonds = fire station locations. Note the spatial mismatch: highest fire activity clusters in the northern periphery where station density is lowest.



Binary access classification: red = Under-served (>10km from nearest station), blue = Within 10km. Under-served municipalities concentrate in the northwestern and southeastern periphery — precisely where historical fire exposure is highest.



Multi-criteria priority classification. Four municipalities (dark red) simultaneously exhibit upper-quartile fire exposure, under-served access, and above-median population density.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Key Findings
Four priority municipalities were identified — three in northwestern Attica bordering the Parnitha and Penteli mountain ranges, one in the eastern sector encompassing communities directly impacted by the 2018 Mati fires. These four municipalities account for approximately 8–12% of Attica’s residents outside the central Athens urban core, yet face compounded vulnerability from both high fire exposure and limited emergency service proximity.
The analysis confirms a consistent spatial pattern: the fire station network concentrates where fire exposure is lowest (central Athens and coastal corridor), while the high-exposure periphery where Mati-like conditions exist remains under-served. Positioning even one or two additional stations within or adjacent to the priority municipalities could substantially reduce response times for historically fire-prone areas.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Policy Implications
Strategic positioning of AEGIS resources toward the identified priority municipalities would directly address the coverage gaps that the 2018 disaster exposed. Greece’s new OroraTech satellite monitoring system offers unprecedented early detection capability — but early detection yields limited benefit if ground-based response assets remain 10+ kilometers from high-risk areas. The spatial analysis provides a transparent, evidence-based framework for allocation decisions that shifts planning from politically-driven resource distribution toward data-informed vulnerability targeting.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text:
Limitations
Straight-line distance metrics don’t capture road network complexity, terrain obstacles, or actual drive times — particularly relevant in mountainous Attica. A binary 10km threshold doesn’t differentiate between municipalities 11km and 25km from a station. Future extensions could incorporate network analysis, graduated access classifications, and social vulnerability indices beyond population density.

title: Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece
text: Files

Paper & Code

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
← Back to Research



Course: Quantitative Analyses of Public Policy Issues Instructor: Prof. Gaurav Khanna Term: Winter 2026 Tools: R · metafor · J-PAL/IZA Data
A consistent negative gradient: Lower electricity access is associated with larger learning gains from adaptive ed-tech, after controlling for intervention type. The program that is hardest to deploy — in the places with the least infrastructure — appears to be most valuable precisely there.
The Mindspark analysis: The program does not widen gaps. Both low-SES and low-baseline students show substantial gains, though heterogeneity estimates are imprecise given sample size.
The implication: Adaptive ed-tech may be most valuable precisely where it is least commonly deployed.

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
The Question
Do educational technology interventions narrow or widen achievement gaps? And does the answer depend on where you are — specifically, on the infrastructure context of the country where the program is deployed?

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
Why It Matters
Educational technology has been proposed as a scalable solution to persistent learning inequality — particularly in low- and middle-income countries where teacher quality, classroom resources, and student baseline levels vary dramatically. But the evidence is stubbornly mixed. Some programs produce gains of half a standard deviation or more; others show null or negative effects.
Understanding why requires moving past average effects to examine heterogeneity — who benefits, under what conditions, and in what contexts.

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
Methodology
This paper combines two complementary analyses:

Part I: Cross-National Mini Meta-Analysis
I assembled a dataset of 8 randomized controlled trials of ed-tech interventions across three countries (India, USA, Kiribati), extracting standardized effect sizes (Cohen’s d) from published results and merging with World Bank country-level infrastructure covariates.
Key methodological decision: I identified that two U.S. studies (SimCalc) used curriculum-aligned outcome tests — assessments designed specifically around the intervention content — rather than independent standardized assessments used in all other studies. This distinction was invisible in summary statistics but turned out to be the dominant confounder in the meta-regression. Catching it required reading every study’s methods section carefully.
I then ran a weighted random-effects meta-regression in R using the metafor package:
# Primary specification
rma(yi = effect_size, 
    sei = se,
    mods = ~ electricity_access + curriculum_aligned,
    data = meta_dat,
    weights = 1/se^2)


Part II: Mindspark Microdata Heterogeneity Analysis
Using the public replication dataset from Muralidharan, Singh & Ganimian (2019) via the J-PAL/IZA archive (n=535 students across 15 Delhi government schools), I estimated treatment-by-subgroup interaction regressions:
# SES heterogeneity specification
lm(endline_score ~ treatment * low_ses + baseline_score + strata_fe,
   data = mindspark)

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
Key Finding

[Forest plot: Effect sizes across 8 RCTs with 95% CIs — add your image here] ::: {.visual-caption} Forest plot of standardized effect sizes (Cohen’s d) across 8 RCTs. Studies ordered by effect size magnitude. The two SimCalc studies (curriculum-aligned assessments) are visibly higher than the rest.

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
What I Learned
The most important moment in this project was not a regression output — it was recognizing that the SimCalc studies were measuring something different. That’s a data validation judgment that no automated pipeline would catch. It changed the entire interpretation of the meta-regression and illustrates why careful reading of study methodology is as important as the quantitative analysis itself.

title: Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?
text:
Code & Files
View on GitHub
:::

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K
text:
← Back to Research



Type: Personal Project Date: December 2025 Tools: R · ggplot2 · Excel

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K
text:
The Question
After finishing the Honolulu Start to Park 10K, I wanted to know exactly how my family and I performed relative to our respective age and gender groups — not just our raw finishing times.

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K
text:
What I Did
Downloaded the official race results, built a custom dataset in Excel, and wrote an R script to:

Parse chip times from Excel datetime format into minutes
Calculate within-age-group percentile rankings
Produce distribution histograms with individual result markers

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    chip_min = as.numeric(ChipTime - floor_date(ChipTime, "day"), 
                          units = "mins")
  ) %>%
  arrange(chip_min) %>%
  mutate(
    rank_time = row_number(),
    pct_time  = rank_time / n()
  )

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K
text:
Result
Top 24th percentile, M25–29. My parents were equally pleased with their results.

[Histogram: M25-29 finishing time distribution with your result marked — add your image here] ::: {.visual-caption} Distribution of M25–29 finishing times. Orange line marks James Avramis at ~64.3 minutes.

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K
text:
Why It’s Here
This project isn’t technically impressive. But it illustrates something I believe about data work: the instinct to find a question and build the analysis yourself — even when nobody asked you to — is the thing that actually makes someone good at this. I crossed the finish line and immediately wanted to know more than the race app would tell me. So I built the tool to find out.

title: Race Results Analysis — Honolulu Start to Park 10K

title: Max Avramis
text:
Overview
This project is currently in progress. Full write-up coming Spring 2026.
Building a GIS-driven weighted prioritization framework across 174 countries to identify which global markets represent the highest-value private-sector partnership opportunities for the Health Forecasting Alliance (HFA) — a network-of-networks connecting regional health data across 37 countries for global pandemic preparedness.

title: Max Avramis
text:
Deliverables

10 Country Market Profiles — scored on digital health ecosystem maturity, CSR landscape, regulatory environment, and infrastructure readiness
Corporate Targeting Matrix — 15–20 multinationals scored on growth trajectory, CSR history, and HFA strategic fit
GIS Data Package — QGIS-ready datasets (CSV + XLSX) with weighted composite scores across 174 countries
Methodology White Paper — documenting the multi-criteria scoring framework and its broader applicability

title: Max Avramis
text:
Background: HFA Private-Sector Strategy (Capstone, 2026)
This project extends my GPS capstone work with VitalWave CEO Brooke Partridge. The capstone identified pharma, development finance, and tech/AI as HFA’s priority private-sector targets through stakeholder interviews and five forces analysis. This independent project adds the spatial dimension: where should HFA focus first?

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
← Back to Research



Course: Advanced GIS & Remote Sensing Instructor: Prof. Morgan Levy Term: Winter 2026 Tools: Google Earth Engine · CHIRPS · Sentinel-1 SAR · Dynamic World · QGIS
:::
:::

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
The Question
Can satellite observations help evaluate and update FEMA’s outdated flood hazard maps? And what do those maps miss — specifically in a community that just experienced a catastrophic flood?

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
Why It Matters
On July 4, 2025, a catastrophic flash flood struck Kerr County, Texas — killing 27+ people at Camp Mystic, many in structures that were outside FEMA’s mapped flood zones. FEMA maps had not been substantially updated in decades. They were built on historical river hydrology data, with no account for rainfall intensity patterns, urban expansion, or climate change.
This is not a Kerr County problem. Approximately 75% of U.S. flood insurance maps are considered outdated. The binary SFHA zoning framework — you’re either in the flood zone or you’re not — leaves millions of at-risk properties uninsured and countless communities underprepared.

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
Methodology
We used four satellite-derived datasets, all accessed via Google Earth Engine, to interrogate FEMA’s existing maps from multiple angles:








Dataset
Source
What it measures




CHIRPS precipitation
UCSB Climate Hazards Group
Daily rainfall estimates, 1981–present


Sentinel-1 SAR
ESA Copernicus
Flood extent via radar backscatter change


Dynamic World
Google/WRI
Land cover change, urban expansion


JRC GLOFAS
European Commission JRC
Modeled 100-year flood hazard zones


FEMA NFHL
FEMA
Regulatory Special Flood Hazard Areas



Key workflows: - Wrote JavaScript scripts in GEE to filter CHIRPS by date range and extract daily precipitation grids - Ran Sentinel-1 pre/post-event backscatter change detection (June 29 → July 4, 2025) at 10m resolution - Used Dynamic World to quantify urban expansion in Kerr County: ~13% built-area growth from 2016–2025 - Overlaid all layers with JRC 100-year modeled flood zones and FEMA SFHA boundaries

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
Key Finding

[Map: CHIRPS precipitation, July 3–7 2025 vs. JRC 100-year flood corridor — add your image here] ::: {.visual-caption} The heaviest precipitation during the July 2025 event fell outside the JRC-modeled flood hazard corridor — a structural gap between where rain falls and where regulatory risk is mapped.

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
What I Found
Three structural gaps in FEMA’s approach:

Precipitation is not modeled. FEMA maps predict where water goes after entering river channels — not where rainfall is heaviest. CHIRPS shows dramatic within-county variation that FEMA’s maps can’t capture.
Urban expansion is invisible to static maps. A 13% increase in built area over a decade changes runoff dynamics fundamentally. FEMA has no mechanism to update for this without a formal, expensive remapping process.
Binary zoning creates false safety. The SFHA/non-SFHA distinction communicates certainty where none exists. Many of the Camp Mystic structures were outside the SFHA — and therefore uninsured.

title: Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX
text:
Future Research Direction
The natural next step — which I identified in the project’s future research section — is a multi-criteria flood susceptibility index combining precipitation intensity, topography (DEM), urban expansion, and regulatory boundaries. This is the same MCDA framework I applied to the Attica wildfire context, now translated to flash flood risk. That project is underway.

title: Max Avramis
text:
Overview
Policy analysis using original BLS QCEW data to quantify AI’s emerging impact on performing arts employment. Analyzes regulatory capture dynamics in the SAG-AFTRA context and argues that collective bargaining is structurally insufficient as the primary AI governance mechanism — a federal statutory baseline is required.
Full write-up coming soon.
:::

title: Making complex arguments
text:
Policy Researcher · Spatial Analyst · Data Scientist


Making complex arguments
legible to anyone in the room.

I’m Max Avramis — a graduate researcher at UC San Diego’s School of Global Policy and Strategy combining GIS, machine learning, and policy analysis to turn messy, multi-source data into decisions that actually land.


View Research About Me







Featured Projects




🔥 Attica Wildfire MCDA



GIS · Policy Analysis


Wildfire Vulnerability Mapping, Attica Greece


Multi-criteria decision analysis integrating 24 years of fire incident data, emergency service proximity, and population density to identify infrastructure investment priorities for Greece’s €2.1B AEGIS program.


QGISRMCDASpatial Analysis





🌊 Kerr County Flood Risk



Remote Sensing · Policy


Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX


Satellite-based audit of FEMA flood maps following the catastrophic July 4, 2025 Camp Mystic flash flood. Used CHIRPS, Sentinel-1 SAR, and Dynamic World to expose structural gaps in regulatory risk mapping.


Google Earth EngineCHIRPSSentinel-1 SARQGIS





📊 Ed-Tech Meta-Analysis



Quantitative Research · Policy


Does Ed-Tech Narrow or Widen Achievement Gaps?


Cross-national meta-analysis of 8 RCTs testing whether adaptive ed-tech effects vary with national infrastructure. Found a consistent negative gradient: lower electricity access → larger learning gains.


R · metaforJ-PAL/IZA DataMeta-regression





⚙️ Labor Action ML



Machine Learning · Big Data


Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events


ML pipeline comparing logistic regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost on the Cornell Labor Action Tracker (2021–2025), merged with DCI, SDI, and IRS socioeconomic data at ZIP level.


R · caretXGBoostRandom ForestAUC/ROC





🌍 HFA GIS Strategy



GIS · Global Health · In Progress


174-Country Prioritization Framework for the Health Forecasting Alliance


Independent research project (Spring 2026) building a GIS-driven weighted MCDA framework to identify which global markets represent the highest-value private-sector partnership opportunities for HFA.


QGISRMCDAFive Forces





🏃 Honolulu 10K



Personal · R · Excel


Race Results Analysis, Honolulu 10K 2025


After finishing the Honolulu Start to Park 10K, I downloaded the race data and built my own dataset to see exactly how my family and I placed within our respective age and gender groups. Top 24th percentile, M25–29.


R · ggplot2ExcelData Wrangling
